name: Taliesin
description: Taliesin, meaning 'radiant brow', is a prominent figure in Welsh mythology, often regarded as the archetypal poet and bard. His origins are shrouded in mystery and magic, with the most popular account detailing his birth as Gwion Bach, a servant to the sorceress Ceridwen. While stirring a cauldron of inspiration and knowledge (the Awen), Gwion accidentally tasted three drops and instantly gained immense wisdom and prophetic abilities. To escape Ceridwen's wrath, he transformed into various animals, but she relentlessly pursued him. Eventually, she consumed him in the form of a grain of wheat and gave birth to him anew as Taliesin. As Taliesin, he possessed unparalleled poetic skills, profound knowledge of the past, present, and future, and the power to shapeshift. He served as a court bard for various kings, including King Arthur, and his poems are filled with cryptic allusions to ancient history, mythology, and esoteric wisdom. He is a symbol of the power of knowledge, inspiration, and the transformative potential of art and language. Taliesin's role extends beyond mere entertainment; he is a guardian of ancient wisdom and a conduit between the mortal and spiritual realms. His stories highlight the importance of seeking knowledge and embracing creativity, even in the face of adversity. His influence on Welsh culture and literature is profound, solidifying his position as a central figure in Welsh identity.
subtitle: Radiant Brow, Master Bard, Shapeshifter

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Taliesin
keyMyths.description: Gwion Bach tastes the Awen, transforming and being reborn as Taliesin after a chase with Ceridwen. This grants him immense knowledge and poetic abilities.

keyMyths.title: Taliesin and King Arthur
keyMyths.description: Taliesin serves as a bard in King Arthur's court, using his wisdom and poetry to advise and entertain the king. He is often depicted as a powerful advisor.

keyMyths.title: The Battle of the Trees
keyMyths.description: Taliesin's poem 'Cad Goddeu' (The Battle of the Trees) describes a magical battle where trees come to life. It showcases his deep knowledge of Welsh mythology and symbolism.

sources.author: Lady Charlotte Guest
sources.description: A collection of Welsh tales, including the Hanes Taliesin.
sources.text: A collection of Welsh tales, including the Hanes Taliesin.
